en.extract:
The brush bronzewing (Phaps elegans) is a species of bird in the pigeon family, Columbidae. It is endemic to Australia, with two biogeographically distinct subspecies.


Taxonomy
The brush bronzewing is one of around 310 species in the family Columbidae and can be classified into two distinct subspecies, P. elegans elegans and P. elegans occidentalis. P. elegans was first described by C. J. Temminck in 1809.


Description
The brush bronzewing is similar in size and shape to the closely related common bronzewing (Phaps chalcoptera), however it's shorter and stockier in appearance.
These birds are relatively small and range in size from 25 to 33 cm. Sexual dimorphism is apparent in these birds. Both sexes are dark-olive brown on top, rich chestnut in colour along the nape and shoulder with blue-grey underparts. The brush bronzewing is named for the iridescent bars of blue and green across the inner secondary feather of each wing.


Distribution and habitat
The brush bronzewing is endemic to Australia, found in the South-West and South-East of the mainland with populations in Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, Western Australia and also Tasmania. The subspecies P. elegans occidentalis occurs as a geographically distinct population, in the southwest of WA.
This species favours dense coastal heathland, wet or dry sclerophyll forests, woodlands and some mallee areas. Habitats with dense shrub layers and foliage, including native species such as Banksia, Acacia, Melaleuca or Leptospermum, allow these cautious birds to find cover.


Behaviour


Feeding
This bird feeds on seeds, berries and small insects; foraging primarily on the ground Like most granivores, the brush bronzewing will swallow small pieces of grit and pebble to aid the grinding of seeds within the gizzard. Local movements are likely a response to food and habitat availability. Unlike the common bronzewing, feeding doesn't occur in small flocks, usually with birds foraging singly or in pairs. Also similarly to that species, the brush bronzewing drinks at dawn or dusk, approaching watering holes cautiously after landing a short distance away.


Breeding
The Brush Bronzewing exhibits a breeding display similar to the common bronzewing, including a courtship display of bowing and a vocal advertisement of a low repeated 'hoop' or 'whoo' call. Breeding occurs mainly between September - January, however incubation of eggs and hatchlings has been observed all year round. Young are semi-altricial and nidicolous, requiring warmth, nourishment and a high level of parental care. The clutch usually consists of two eggs, which will be incubated for roughly 17 days and fledge after 16–20 days. Adult pairs will resume breeding 3–4 weeks after fledglings become independent.
Due to the shy and cautious nature of these birds, they will flush from their nests abruptly when they perceive a potential threat. This may be the cause of their low nesting success (~10%), with one study finding that seven young hatched from 29 eggs across 15 nests, of which only three survived to fledge.


Migration
Birds tend to reside within their local habitats, with no long distance banding recoveries being and no large scale migrations apparent. In a study from 1967, one bird was recovered just 25 km from the initial banding site. These birds are rarely seen in flocking formation, preferring to move singly, in pairs or small family groups; the largest recorded gathering was a group of eight birds.


Threats and conservation
The brush bronzewing is listed as a species of Least Concern on the IUCN Red List and considered by BirdLife Australia to have secure populations in all states it occurs in.
Like many other ground-dwelling bird species, the bronze brushwing is threatened by introduced predators such as cats and foxes. Like several other species, it appears to have increased toxicity to non-native mammalian predators due to feeding on Gastrolobium plants, which may contribute to the maintenance of biodiversity in south-west Western Australia.


References

The '''brush bronzewing''' ('''''Phaps elegans''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the pigeon family, [[Columbidae]]. It is endemic to [[Australia]], with two biogeographically distinct subspecies.

== Taxonomy ==
The brush bronzewing is one of around 310 species in the family [[Columbidae]]<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1 = Larcombe|first1 = A. N.|last2 = Withers|first2 = P. C.|last3 = Maloney|first3 = S. K.|date = 2003|title = Thermoregulatory physiology of the Crested Pigeon Ocyphaps lophotes and the Brush Bronzewing Phaps elegans|doi =10.1007/s00360-002-0323-7 |pmid = 12743724|journal = Journal of Comparative Physiology B|volume = 173|issue = 3|pages = 215–222|s2cid = 6750633}}</ref> and can be classified into two distinct subspecies, ''P. elegans elegans'' and ''P. elegans occidentalis''.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title = Field Guide to the Birds of Australia|author = Simpson|author2 = Day|name-list-style=amp|publisher = Penguin Random House|year = 2010|location = Australia|page = 132}}</ref> ''P. elegans'' was first described by [[Coenraad Jacob Temminck|C. J. Temminck]] in 1809.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Gasso Miracle|first=M. E.|date=2008|title=The significance of Temminck's work on Biogeography: Early nineteenth century natural history in Leiden, the Netherlands|journal=Journal of the History of Biology|volume=41|issue=4|pages=677–716|doi=10.1007/s10739-008-9158-8|pmid=19244845}}</ref>

== Description ==
The brush bronzewing is similar in size and shape to the closely related [[common bronzewing]] (''Phaps chalcoptera),'' however it's shorter and stockier in appearance.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title = Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds (HANZAB) Vol 3. Snipe to Pigeons|author = P. J. Higgins|author2 = S. J. J. F. Davies|name-list-style=amp|publisher = Oxford University Press|year = 1996|location = Oxford}}</ref>

These birds are relatively small and range in size from 25 to 33 cm.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> [[Sexual dimorphism]] is apparent in these birds. Both sexes are dark-olive brown on top, rich chestnut in colour along the nape and shoulder with blue-grey underparts.<ref name=":1" /> The brush bronzewing is named for the iridescent bars of blue and green across the inner secondary feather of each wing.<ref name=":1" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
The brush bronzewing is [[Endemism|endemic]] to Australia, found in the South-West and South-East of the mainland with populations in Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, Western Australia and also Tasmania.<ref name=":1" /> The subspecies ''P. elegans occidentalis'' occurs as a geographically distinct population, in the southwest of WA.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/species/Phaps-elegans|title=Birds in Backyards|author=BirdLife Australia|website=Brush Bronzewing|publisher=BirdLife Australia|access-date=5 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160313111614/http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/species/Phaps-elegans|archive-date=13 March 2016}}</ref>

This species favours dense coastal heathland, wet or dry [[Sclerophyll|sclerophyll forests]], woodlands and some mallee areas.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> Habitats with dense shrub layers and foliage, including native species such as ''[[Banksia]], [[Acacia]], [[Melaleuca]]'' or ''[[Leptospermum]],'' allow these cautious birds to find cover.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />

== Behaviour ==

=== Feeding ===
This bird feeds on seeds, berries and small insects; foraging primarily on the ground<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Frith|first=H. J.|date=1952|title=Notes on the pigeons of the Richmond River NSW|journal=Emu|volume=52|issue=2|pages=89–99|doi=10.1071/mu952089}}</ref> Like most [[granivore]]s, the brush bronzewing will swallow small pieces of grit and pebble to aid the grinding of seeds within the [[gizzard]]. Local movements are likely a response to food and habitat availability.<ref name=":1" /> Unlike the [[common bronzewing]], feeding doesn't occur in small flocks, usually with birds foraging singly or in pairs.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/species/Phaps-chalcoptera|title=Common Bronzewing|author=BirdLife Australia|website=Birds in Backyard|access-date=5 April 2016|archive-date=19 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181119210929/http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/species/Phaps-chalcoptera}}</ref> Also similarly to that species, the brush bronzewing drinks at dawn or dusk, approaching watering holes cautiously after landing a short distance away.<ref name=":3" />

=== Breeding ===
The Brush Bronzewing exhibits a breeding display similar to the common bronzewing, including a courtship display of bowing and a vocal advertisement of a low repeated 'hoop' or 'whoo' call.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" /> Breeding occurs mainly between September - January, however incubation of eggs and hatchlings has been observed all year round. Young are semi-[[altricial]] and [[nidicolous]], requiring warmth, nourishment and a high level of parental care.<ref name=":1" /> The clutch usually consists of two eggs, which will be incubated for roughly 17 days and fledge after 16–20 days.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" /> Adult pairs will resume breeding 3–4 weeks after fledglings become independent.

Due to the shy and cautious nature of these birds, they will flush from their nests abruptly when they perceive a potential threat. This may be the cause of their low nesting success (~10%), with one study finding that seven young hatched from 29 eggs across 15 nests, of which only three survived to fledge.<ref name=":1" />

=== Migration ===
Birds tend to reside within their local habitats, with no long distance banding recoveries being and no large scale [[Bird migration|migrations]] apparent. In a study from 1967, one bird was recovered just 25 km from the initial banding site.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" /> These birds are rarely seen in flocking formation, preferring to move singly, in pairs or small family groups; the largest recorded gathering was a group of eight birds.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" />

== Threats and conservation ==
The brush bronzewing is listed as a species of [[least-concern species|Least Concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]] and considered by [[BirdLife Australia]] to have secure populations in all states it occurs in.<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /><ref name=":3" />

Like many other ground-dwelling bird species, the bronze brushwing is threatened by introduced predators such as cats and foxes. Like several other species, it appears to have increased toxicity to non-native mammalian predators due to feeding on ''[[Gastrolobium]]'' plants, which may contribute to the maintenance of biodiversity in south-west Western Australia.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Peacock|first1=D. E.|last2=Christensen|first2=P. E.|date=2011|title=Historical accounts of toxicity to introduced carnivores consuming Brush Bronzewing pigeons (Phaps elegans) and other vertebrate fauna in south-west Western Australia|journal=Australian Zoologist|volume=35|issue=3|pages=826–842|doi=10.7882/az.2011.034}}</ref>
